introduction.p: Consider the functions f and g defined by the following figures. Assume that the given lines and curves pass through intersection points on the grid when it looks plausible. For instance, (0,2.5) and (3,-0.5) lie on the graph of f, and (-1,3) and (1.5, 1.5) lie on the graph of g.

description.p: ADD ALT TEXT TO THIS IMAGE
ADD ALT TEXT TO THIS IMAGE

solution.p: From the graphs, f(0) = \frac{5}{2} and g(0) = 1, so (f+g)(0) = \frac{5}{2} + 1 = \frac{7}{2}.
From the graphs, f(1) = 4 and g(1) = 3, so (g-f)(1) = 3 - 4 = -1.

statement.p: Determine the exact value of (f \cdot g)(-1).

solution.p: From the graphs, f(-1) = 3 and g(-1) = 3, so (f \cdot g)(-1) = 3 \cdot 3 = 9.

statement.p: Are there any values of x for which \left( \frac{f}{g} \right)(x) is undefined? If not, explain why. If so, determine the values and justify your answer.

solution.p: \left(\frac{f}{g}\right)(x) is undefined wherever g(x) = 0. From the graph, g(x) = 0 at x = -2 and x = 3.

statement.p: For what values of x is (f \cdot g)(x) = 0? Why?

solution.p: (f \cdot g)(x) = 0 wherever f(x) = 0 or g(x) = 0. From the graphs, f(x) = 0 at x = \frac{7}{3}, and g(x) = 0 at x = -2 and x = 3. So (f \cdot g)(x) = 0 at x \in \left\{-2,\, \frac{7}{3},\, 3\right\}.

statement.p: Are there any values of x for which (f-g)(x) = 0? Why or why not?

solution.p:
Yes: (f-g)(x) = 0 when f(x) = g(x). One intersection is visible from the graphs at x = -1. On the interval -1 \lt x \le 1, the formulas are f(x) = \frac{5}{2} + x and g(x) = -x^2 + 4; setting them equal gives:
x^2 + x - \frac{3}{2} &= 0 x &= \frac{-1 + \sqrt{7}}{2}.
So (f-g)(x) = 0 at x = -1 and x = \dfrac{\sqrt{7}-1}{2}.
